questions.question: Natural selection requires variation within a population. This variation primarily comes from:
options: Random mutations in DNA and genetic recombination
Environmental changes that alter organisms directly
questions.explanation: Genetic variation comes from: (1) Random mutations (DNA copying errors, radiation), (2) Genetic recombination (crossing over during meiosis), (3) Gene flow between populations. Natural selection then acts on this existing variation.

questions.question: Evolution always produces organisms that are perfectly adapted to their environment.
questions.explanation: Evolution produces 'good enough' not 'perfect'. Constraints: historical contingency (stuck with inherited body plans), trade-offs (speed vs. endurance), genetic drift (random changes in small populations), and changing environments.

questions.question: Structures in different species that share a common evolutionary origin (e.g., bat wing and human arm) are called ___ structures.
questions.explanation: Homologous structures share ancestry (bat wing, whale flipper, human arm — all from same ancestral tetrapod limb). Analogous structures have similar function but different origin (bird wing vs. insect wing — convergent evolution).

questions.question: Match each evolutionary mechanism to its description:

right: Random changes in allele frequency (stronger in small populations)
Movement of genes between populations via migration
Traits that increase mating success even at survival cost

questions.explanation: Selection: adaptive (the fittest survive). Drift: random (a flood kills individuals regardless of fitness). Gene flow: migration introduces new alleles. Sexual selection: peacock tails are costly but attractive.

questions.question: Antibiotic resistance is evolving faster than new drugs are developed. By 2050, resistant infections could kill 10M/year. What's the evolutionary explanation and solution?

scenario.context: Bacteria evolve resistance within years. Drug development takes 10-15 years.
findings: Horizontal gene transfer spreads resistance genes between species
Sub-lethal antibiotic doses accelerate resistance evolution
Only 2 new antibiotic classes discovered since 1987

options: Bacteria mutate intentionally in response to antibiotics — they 'learn' to resist
Strong selection pressure (antibiotics kill susceptible bacteria) + short generation time + horizontal gene transfer = rapid evolution. Solution: reduce selection pressure + phage therapy
Resistance genes are pre-existing; antibiotics just reveal them — stop using antibiotics entirely
Only hospital-acquired infections are evolving resistance
questions.explanation: Antibiotics create extreme selection pressure: resistant individuals survive and reproduce (no competition). With 20-minute generation times and horizontal gene transfer, resistance spreads exponentially. Solutions: antibiotic stewardship, phage therapy, CRISPR-based approaches.

questions.question: Should humans use gene drives to deliberately drive disease-carrying mosquito species to extinction?
questions.scenario: Gene drives could eliminate Anopheles gambiae (malaria vector, kills 600K/year). But: ecosystem effects unknown, irreversible once released, crosses national borders without consent.

options.label: Yes — 600K deaths/year justifies eliminating one species; humans have caused extinctions before
options.framework: Utilitarian — lives saved outweigh ecological risk

options.label: No — we can't predict ecosystem cascading effects; playing god with irreversible technology
Suppress (reduce population) but don't eliminate — maintain reversibility
options.framework: Proportional intervention — reduce harm without elimination

options.label: Only with global consensus — gene drives cross borders and affect all nations
options.framework: Global governance — collective decision for collective intervention

questions.explanation: Population suppression (not elimination) maintains ecological reversibility while reducing disease burden. Self-limiting gene drives that decay over generations provide a middle path. Full elimination is irreversible and ecologically unpredictable.
